hook: Specialty coffee bar inside a restored Victorian gents' urinal
facts: Built in the 1890s as underground public toilets on Foley Street
Closed in the 1960s and boarded up for around 50 years
Reopened 2013 with a marble counter set through the old porcelain urinals
Original green-and-white wall tiles and cisterns retained
w: Descend the steps off Foley Street and you order a flat white where Victorian gents once stood: an 1890s underground urinal, shut in the 1960s, boarded up for half a century. They slung a marble counter through the porcelain stalls and kept the green tiles. Coffee, served below ground, between the cisterns.

hook: Tiny vegan coffee bar wedged into a live railway arch
facts: Opened 2012 directly beneath Kentish Town West Overground station
Named for Tom Bolton's book on London's buried villages
Dropped all animal products from the menu in 2017
Seats roughly a dozen people under the brick vault
w: Trains rumble overhead as you sip, because the whole room sits inside a live railway arch under Kentish Town West. A dozen seats, brick vault, named for Tom Bolton's book on the lost villages buried under the city. Went fully vegan in 2017. Coffee in the fields beneath the rails.

hook: Specialty coffee inside a working Christopher Wren church
facts: Set inside St Nicholas Cole Abbey, rebuilt by Wren after the Great Fire
Opened March 2014 and named for the church's architect
Won best new London coffee shop in its opening year
Pours beans from King's Cross roaster Caravan
w: Order at the nave: this coffee bar trades inside St Nicholas Cole Abbey, the church Christopher Wren rebuilt after the Great Fire flattened the medieval one. Named for its architect, it opened in 2014 and took best new London coffee shop that year. Caravan beans, poured under a Wren ceiling.

hook: Closet-sized coffee bar that fuelled London's specialty wave
facts: Opened 2009 on Great Ormond Street, opposite the children's hospital
One of central London's earliest third-wave espresso bars
Standing-room interior barely larger than a cupboard
Long a refuelling stop for hospital staff and visiting parents
w: Barely wider than a phone box, this 2009 espresso bar helped drag London out of its burnt-flat-white years. It sits opposite Great Ormond Street hospital, so the queue mixes worn-out parents, surgeons on a ten-minute break and coffee obsessives. No room to sit. The flat white earns the squeeze.

n: Department of Coffee and Social Affairs
hook: The founding shop of a homegrown London coffee mini-empire
facts: First branch opened late 2010 on Leather Lane market
Fitted into a former ironmonger's, name nodding to bureaucracy
Grew from this single site into a chain of London cafes
Sits amid the lunchtime street-food stalls of Leather Lane
w: Everything started here, in a former ironmonger's on Leather Lane in 2010, the deadpan civil-service name a joke about London's coffee bureaucracy. From this one shop sprang a whole estate of cafes. Step out into the lunchtime market crush of frying onions and fake watches. The original department.

hook: Pioneering Wardour Street roaster from London's early specialty days
facts: Opened on Wardour Street as Tapped & Packed around 2009
Among Soho's first dedicated third-wave coffee bars
Long signposted shops by number rather than name
Roasts and brews single origins alongside a house espresso
w: Back when Soho still ran on instant and froth, this Wardour Street room opened as Tapped and Packed and started taking beans seriously. One of the first proper specialty bars in the West End, fond of numbering its shops instead of naming them. Roasts its own. An old hand from the early days.

hook: Miniature 1950s Italian espresso and aperitivo bar off Old Compton Street
facts: Opened 2014 by Tony Conigliaro and Illy's Marco Arrigo
Named for and styled after Rome's Termini station
Serves espresso from 7.30am, then Negronis and aperitivo until late
A tiny standing-room counter seating around twenty
w: Cocktail alchemist Tony Conigliaro and Illy's Marco Arrigo built this thumbnail of 1950s Rome off Old Compton Street, named for the city's central station. Espresso from half seven, Negronis decanted from bottles by night. Twenty seats, brass and burgundy. A railway buffet that never leaves Soho.

hook: Scratch-built specialty cafe raised from reclaimed scaffolding
facts: Built late 2011 from reclaimed wood and discarded materials
Opened to the public in 2013 on Stroud Green Road
Counter, tables and chairs were once scaffold boards
Roasts under the Vagabond Coffee Roasters name
w: The counter you lean on used to be scaffolding. Two builders raised this Stroud Green cafe in 2011 from reclaimed wood and skip finds, then started roasting their own. Tables, chairs, the whole fit-out had a previous life holding up someone's wall. Coffee that began as a pile of salvage.

hook: All-day brunch and on-site roastery in a vast Victorian granary
facts: Opened 2012, the first restaurant in regenerated Granary Square
Housed in the Grade II Eastern Transit Shed grain store
Roasts its own beans on site in the dining room
Built its name on globally-spiced all-day brunch plates
w: First through the door when King's Cross reinvented itself, this 2012 canteen fills an old Victorian grain store on Granary Square. A roaster chugs away in the dining room, scenting the brunch of cornbread, kimchi eggs and well-travelled plates. Industrial bones, ducting overhead. Coffee roasted where you eat.

hook: The original branch of London's everyman all-day brunch institution
facts: Opened 2005 on D'Arblay Street, the first of the group
Named and themed around the 1980s John Hughes film
Famed for queues and stacked American-diner breakfasts
Some branches hide a speakeasy behind a fridge door
legend: They say a SmegHead password at certain branches opens a bar concealed behind a fridge
w: The mothership of London's brunch boom started here on D'Arblay Street in 2005, all John Hughes nostalgia and pancake stacks the size of hubcaps. The queue down the pavement became part of the furniture. Later branches hid speakeasies behind fridge doors. Where the city learned to brunch.

hook: Andean picanteria serving quinoa-fed Peruvian weekend brunch
facts: From Martin Morales, who brought Peruvian food to mainstream London
Relaunched at a larger Redchurch Street site in 2024
Brunch leans on superfoods, ceviche and chicharron sandwiches
Named for the Andes and the highland picanteria tradition
w: Martin Morales, who dragged ceviche into the London mainstream, runs this Redchurch Street picanteria on Andean highland cooking. Weekend brunch means quinoa, smashed avocado, chicharron rolls and a pisco sour before noon. Relaunched bigger in 2024. The Andes, transplanted to a Shoreditch corner.

hook: Lakeside park cafe doing Sri Lankan-tinged East End brunch
facts: Sits beside the boating lake in Victoria Park, Tower Hamlets
An early anchor of East London's weekend brunch scene
Small seasonal kitchen with a Sri Lankan accent
Hours stretch and shrink with the park's gates
w: Out by the boating lake in Victoria Park, this little pavilion drew the Hackney brunch crowd long before brunch went citywide. The kitchen runs a tight seasonal menu with a Sri Lankan tilt beside the full Englishes. Opening hours bend to the park gates. Eggs by the water, dogs underfoot.

hook: Pioneering Australian cafe that helped import brunch culture
facts: Opened 2008 on a quiet Charlotte Place pedestrian lane
Among the first to bring Antipodean brunch to central London
Built a cult around its corn fritters with poached eggs
Founded by Australian Shelagh Ryan
w: Tucked down the pedestrian sliver of Charlotte Place, this 2008 Aussie cafe is one of the rooms that taught London to take brunch seriously: flat whites, smashed avo, the famous corn fritters with streaky bacon. Founder Shelagh Ryan brought the Antipodean playbook north. A quiet pioneer.

hook: Modern Australian all-day brunch down a Greek Street passage
facts: Set in Ilona Rose House, reached via a passage off Greek Street
Modern Australian kitchen named for a Sydney swimming spot
Weekend breakfast runs early before the all-day menu
Grew from a Queen's Park original into this larger Soho site
w: Slip down the passage off Greek Street into Ilona Rose House and you reach this modern Australian dining room named for a Sydney swimming cove. Weekend breakfasts of crumpets and confit before the all-day menu kicks in. Grew out of a Queen's Park original. Sunshine cooking under Soho brick.

hook: Flat-cap-themed specialty roaster minutes from Borough Market
facts: Original cafe opened 2014 on Union Street near Borough
Whole brand built on a wry Victorian gentlemen's-club conceit
Staff and signage lean on flat caps and old-fashioned manners
Roasts its own beans and supplies its other London sites
w: A wry conceit runs through the whole place: politeness costs nothing, so the staff wear flat caps and the menu plays Victorian gentlemen's club. The first shop opened on Union Street in 2014, a short walk from Borough Market, and now roasts for the rest. Good manners, properly extracted.
